wikitext:
= Sicilian Defense, Dragon Variation, Yugoslav Attack =
{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|Yugoslav Attack||rd|nd|bd|qd||rd|kd||pd|pd|||pd|pd|bd|pd||||pd||nd|pd|||||||||||||nl|pl||||||nl||bl|pl|||pl|pl|pl||||pl|pl|rl|||ql|kl|bl||rl|parent=[[Chess/Sicilian Dragon|Sicilian Dragon]]}}The Yugoslav Attack continues with 7...O-O. In the Yugoslav, Black castles kingside while White castles queenside, leading to an opposite-side-castling game where speed is important. Black often pushes on the queenside with a5 and b5 and continues advancing those pawns, while White plays g4 and h4 and continues advancing those pawns. The fianchettoed bishop on g7 is a key piece in Black's attack on the queenside and what makes the Dragon the Dragon.

White's most popular continuation is [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...c5/2. Nf3/2...d6/3. d4/3...cxd4/4. Nxd4/4...Nf6/5. Nc3/5...g6/6. Be3/6...Bg7/7. f3/7...O-O/8. Qd2|8. Qd2]], preparing for a queenside castle.

Second is [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...c5/2. Nf3/2...d6/3. d4/3...cxd4/4. Nxd4/4...Nf6/5. Nc3/5...g6/6. Be3/6...Bg7/7. f3/7...O-O/8. Bc4|8. Bc4]], which often transposes to the main line.
